content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

June 23, 1993

Bobby G. Glaser
Attorney at Law
The La Jolla Group
8304 Clairemont Mesa Blvd., Ste. 213
San Diego, CA 92111-1315

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-212

Dear Mr. Glaser:

You have requested advice on behalf of Evonne Schulze for San
Diego City Council, Fred Colby for San Diego City Council, and
Assemblyman Mike Gotch concerning the campaign provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/

QUESTION

Candidates Schulze and Colby and Assemblyman Gotch are
interested in conducting individual polls. A polling firm has
offered a reduced price because all three polls can be combined
using one very large sample. You have asked whether this activity
would be prohibited or would trigger any special reporting
requirements under the Act.

CONCLUSION

Candidates subject to valid contribution limits and
candidates running in a special election are prohibited from
receiving contributions or other transfers of funds from other
officeholders or candidates. In addition, if a candidate receives
a discount or rebate not extended to the public generally, the
candidate has received a reportable contribution.

To the extent that each candidate pays no more than his or
her share of the cost of the poll, and each candidate pays you or
the polling firm directly, no transfer will occur. If the polling
firm provides the discount as part of its standard business policy

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Bobby G. Glaser
Our File No. A-93-212
Page 2


of providing discounts in similar situations and does not provide
the discount for political purposes, the candidates will not
receive a contribution from the polling firm.

FACTS

You represent candidates Evonne Schulze for San Diego City
Council and Fred Colby for San Diego City Council. The City of
San Diego has a contribution limitation ordinance. You also work
for Assemblyman Mike Gotch. All of the candidates are interested
in conducting individual polls. The polling firm with which you
normally contract has offered a reduced price if the three polls
can be combined.

A large part of the Assembly District overlaps the two City
Council districts. The polling firm has proposed conducting all
three polls using one large sample and asking separate sets of
questions for each candidate. Three individual reports will be
prepared and poll results will not be shared among the candidates.
However, by combining the three polls, the cost of each individual
poll will be lowered. Each candidate will pay a proportionate
share of the cost.

ANALYSIS

The Transfer Prohibition

Proposition 73 was adopted by the people of California in
June of 1988. The proposition imposed new restrictions on the use
of campaign funds. (See, Section 85304 and Section 89510(b).)
For example, Section 85304 provides:

No candidate for elective office or committee
controlled by that candidate or candidates for
elective office shall transfer any contribution to
any other candidate for elective office. Transfers
of funds between candidates or their controlled
committees are prohibited.

In the case of Service Employees International Union, AFL-
CIO, CIO, et al. v. Fair Political Practices Commission (9th Cir.
1992) 955 F.2d 1312, cert.den. ___ U.S. ___, 112 S.Ct. 3056, the
court invalidated the fiscal year contribution limitations of the
Act. In addition, the court invalidated the transfer ban of
Section 85304 insofar as it was premised upon the need to prevent
evasion of fiscal year campaign contribution limitations.

Thus, pursuant to the federal court's order, the transfer ban
is invalid where applied to support fiscal year limits. However,
the transfer ban continues to prohibit the transfer of campaign
funds where the prohibition serves to uphold constitutionally
valid contribution limits. Consequently, the transfer ban

Bobby G. Glaser
Our File No. A-93-212
Page 3


prohibits a candidate from transferring campaign funds to (1) any
candidate on the ballot for election in a special election for any
local or state office; and, (2) any candidate in a jurisdiction
with valid local contribution limits. (Riffenburg Advice Letter,
No. A-90-761.)

You have indicated that each candidate will pay the full cost
for his or her share of the poll. Therefore, as long as each
candidate pays you or the polling firm directly, no transfers
between the candidates will occur.

Discounts as Contributions

A "contribution" is a monetary or nonmonetary payment made
for political purposes for which full and adequate consideration
is not made to the donor and includes the granting of discounts or
rebates not extended to the public generally. (Section 82005.)

From the information provided in your letter, it appears that
the candidates will receive a discount in the cost of the poll
because combining the poll will result in lower costs to the
polling firm. To the extent that the polling firm is providing
the discount as part of its standard business policy of passing
cost savings on to its clients and not for political purposes, the
candidates will not receive a contribution from the polling firm.

If you have questions concerning this letter, please contact
me at (916) 322-5662.

Sincerely,

Jeff Marschner
General Counsel

By: Carla Wardlow
Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division
